question.text: What is 'option assignment' and when does it occur?

answer.text: Assignment is the process where an option seller is notified that the option buyer has exercised their right — the seller must fulfill the obligation to buy (put) or sell (call) the underlying. In Indian exchange-traded options (European), assignment only occurs at expiry
Assignment is the process of allocating a new option contract to a market maker
Assignment can happen at any time for any option type in India
Assignment only applies to the option buyer, not the seller

explanation.text: **The Logic:** When an option is exercised, the clearing corporation assigns the obligation to an option seller through a random or proportional allocation process. In India, all exchange-traded options are European style, so assignment only happens at expiry (not before). **The Trap:** In American options (not traded on Indian exchanges), early assignment is possible. Assignment is the seller's obligation, not the buyer's — the buyer exercises, the seller gets assigned. **Cross-Exam Context:** Assignment rules are similar across equity (VIII), currency (I), and commodity (XVI) options in India — all European, so assignment only at expiry. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Since Indian options are European, sellers don't face early assignment risk — this is a significant advantage for option writing strategies compared to American-style options.
